item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this with the random button one day, and I can safely say it's one of my favorite SCPs. It's sad, and it's also open-ended, so what could happen to it is left up to the imagination. It's just unfortunate that there aren't any tales about it. Maybe I could try to make one…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. █████'s entire body [DATA EXPUNGED] violently by an unidentified force

Ooh, I wonder what could have been expunged in there. No, really, why didn't you just write "body integrated into SCP-1030-1?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe name it "Ha-emet Chaim." (In Hebrew, this means "The truth of Life"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely love this. The concept is original, having a golem that can incorporate anything into it's body. Basically, you made a golem that could be considered a cyborg! I also like the mysterious figure referred to as "Maker" (most likely a god, or somebody with semiomnipotence). Additionally, 1030-01 tries to replicate via -02, the fact that it tries to bring others back to life in an area where d-class are constantly culled, all that death and hatred could explain why -03 are dangerous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mark is love mark is life This one was quite good. I kinda feel bad for the l'il golem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one! This is an interesting new take on the traditional golem myth. With its mysterious origins and apparent melancholy, I would love to see a tale based around 1030. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first SCP that I really came to love on this site. I really feel bad for the guy/thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(necropost, I think is the word)
Or they remove the slip of paper with the symbol from its mouth.
I read the same type of thing in an encyclopedia of fantasy stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it have someting to do with SCP 073.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doing so could decomision a SCP not a risk that the foundation would take.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gawd, I hate you. Can't stop humming that now. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite SCP's. The fact you manged to make the golem appear both dangerous and tragic speaks of your skills as a writer, and I appreciated the use the old golem legends (I think "אמת" works fine here, although maybe something like "יציר" (Created) or even "יוצר" (Creator) might also work).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. I thought the bit that the golem wrote at the end was a very nice touch - it ends the article on a tragic note and makes you emphatize with the creature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't have much to add, but I really wanted to say that this is now one of my favourite stories on the site. The way you have something so sad and harmless-seeming suddenly reveal just how horrifically dangerous it is… brilliant…

The addendum at the end doesn't really work for me, seems to be riddled with cliches, but not enough to ruin the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might it be possible that he has more to say but simply has no one to open up to?

Maybe something more helpful towards replicating the mark?

Consider the subject's seemingly painful existence. Everything the golem is composed is, in some way or another, breaking down and decomposing and it apparently hurts. Perhaps a body cast of metal and modular parts would ease the golem's pain. They would degrade, of course, but they wouldn't need to be twisted and mangled to be incorporated into the golem. Provided the force incorporating the pieces would understand where each one went and placed them accordingly.

Perhaps the he'd feel less alone knowing someone is trying to helpful. It might get a few more words out of him.

Just a thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
pardon the necro-post, however, I feel that the current version, is somewhat 'stronger' for using a different word than Emet. Emet was used to bring unliving clay to life, and to return it to unlife by removing one character. That would lead to experiments by SCP staff to try 'turning the golem off' using the same method as the legend.

This 'golem' didn't start ass unliving clay, according to its testimony, it started as a person, so this is a different form of golem creation, instead of a clay robot powered by 'Truth,' it's a person cursed with 'Life.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word אמת is the best choice. That's "Emet", Truth. I was told that when the Rabbi no longer needed the Golem, he erased the first letter, א, leaving מת . That's a way of spelling "Mett" or "Mutt", meaning Death, so the Golem turned back into clay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Consider this another wish that one could upvote comments.
Incidentally, it turns out we have a couple Katamari SCPs. I forget their numbers, but we have a corpse magnet and a guy who fuses with any inanimate objects he touches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a ton for the clarification. It wasn't my intention, but there were, like, five translations for "Life." I think I'll keep this one, because it means I have a name for my golem now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a kinda nice idea, I'm just informing Goose in case that's not what he intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda like the idea that what's keeping the golem alive is a name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word you're using, "חיים", or Chayyim, does have a technical meaning, but its use in Hebrew vernacular is generally as a name. In essence you're writing Bob or George on the back of the Golem's head. As Reject suggested you could use "חַי", which would make much more sense. I'd suggest using "אמת" (truth), as is traditionally used in golem stories, but I think that'd be too cliche. You could also use the Hamsa, but that may be a bit esoteric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just so you know, there's a symbol for life in Hebrew as well as a word that is inscribed on a hell of a lot of Jewish stuff. It's "חַי."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. Someone on chat identified what it was before I had any Hebrew characters or a recovery report, so I don't think I need to spell it out. It's "monster" now.
Even if someone doesn't know it's a golem, oh well. A misshapen form of inanimate material given unnatural life called by any other name is just as sweet. That being said, I'm not changing the designation on the list page. Anyone who gets straight to the article will get to go a-Googling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome. I changed the word I had in there to "Life," or some approximation of it. Thanks. Also, I put in the 1030-3 designation for the Disney's "Beauty and the Beast" cast rejects the golem keeps making. I'd deleted it accidentally in my sandbox draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Small points:

  • SCP-1030-3 is never actually STATED as being the inscribed objects.
  • THIS will let you get better words phrases for a copy paste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The down-side is that you have to listen to the Mission: Impossible theme the entire time. Which is fine until hour 3 and you're two sentences in..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. Literally the only thing that I would change is "reports of a "golem"" to "reports of a monster". The only reason why is that I liked the little feeling of knowing what having Hebrew characters inscribed onto something that should be inanimate, without needing it spelled out. That little Easter egg discovery moment is fun, and I prefer it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude. Now I totally want a Unicode keyboard that I have to be suspended over in a harness to use it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I use a keyboard with hundreds of thousands of individual keys, representing the entire Unicode set. It's approximately the size of a football field, and I have to be suspended over it in a harness to use it.

No, really, I just copy-paste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good ideas. Funny thing about the Hebrew letters - I didn't even know that part of the Golem of Prague myth. I just Googled to see where golems come from, and first thing that popped up was Golem of Prague. I may do that.
And DARNIT. I meant to double-check what "embossed" meant ever since my first draft of this thing, and kept forgetting. Now to learn how you typed those Hebrew characters. (Just kidding; too lazy. I'm gonna copy-paste it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: na naaa nanana nana naa na, katamari SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for the concept.

A few drafting suggestions:

items => tangible objects

constructed of various objects, currently including a pair of shoes… => constructed of various objects. At present, SCP-1030-1 is comprised of a pair of shoes…

Objects integrated into SCP-1030-1 are corroded, rotted, or otherwise corrupted at an accelerated rate. => Objects integrated into SCP-1030-1 corrode, rot or otherwise decompose at an accelerated rate.

but preference is given to objects requiring a minimum of effort. => but preference is given to objects requiring a minimum of physical alteration.

an intricate symbol => a complex symbol [or "a complex glyph"]. (Why not carry out the golem concept further and say that the glyph incorporates the Hebrew letters "אמת")?

that becomes embossed => that is inscribed. ["Embossed" has a specific meaning that I don't think you
mean here.]

explaining why the symbol wasn’t effective when copied by Foundation personnel => explaining the functionality of SCP-1030-2, including the lack of effectiveness of Foundation-produced copies of SCP-1030-2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting… Very interesting indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You spaced out the Description! Yaaay!

I'd have upvoted anyway, but whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like golems. I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgot to say earlier - kudos for classifying an object from my home country.
